next_head_excerpt:
# Chapter 14

By Monday morning Nate had a legal pad full of arrows, three photocopied site plans, and a headache from trying to learn commercial redevelopment the way he had once learned why a dentist's office could print from one machine but not the other: not by understanding the whole system, exactly, but by tracing every dependency until the thing coughed up a shape.

He had spent the week after Mort's store in Dominic's sent mail, in zoning packets, in rent rolls, in the Eastbridge file, in photocopies that came out of the office machine warm and smelling faintly of burnt dust. He had found Dominic's old arguments in pieces. An email from March about relocation assistance. A margin note on a site plan in Dominic's handwriting, a quick slanted block of capitals next to the end unit: PRESERVE PHASE 1? A yellow sticky on a demolition schedule with the adhesive gone dry enough that it came off in Nate's hand. Somebody had written "holiday optics" on it and underlined it twice. Dominic or Ray or Victor or some lawyer Nate would never meet. It did not matter. The whole office was full of fingerprints that were not fingerprints, traces of people making choices and then leaving the paper behind.

His revised proposal sat in a manila folder on Dominic's desk with a title page he had printed twice because the first one skewed in the copier: EASTBRIDGE RETAIL TRANSITION PLAN. The title sounded like bullshit, which was probably how he knew it belonged in this building. Underneath the title page was a cover memo, then the marked-up footprint, then a page of rough numbers showing w...
open_hooks_to_carry: The hardware store interior: sawdust, WD-40, the wall of specialty fasteners — sensory memory colliding with present experience
Buying the level: Nate holding an object that connects him to childhood
prev_tail_excerpt:
He sat up.

For a second he thought he might actually do it. He put his feet on the floor, crossed the bedroom, and opened the door.

The lamp was still on in the living room. Sarah was a shape under the blanket, turned away, one shoulder making a small ridge. Her tote bag sat on the floor beside the couch, one paper halfway out like a tongue.

He stepped into the hall. The floorboard near the bathroom creaked.

"What?" she said.

Her voice was tired, not inviting, not curious.

He put his hand on the wall. He had come out to tell the truth. He could feel the words lined up wrong in his throat.

"Sarah," he said.

She waited.

He saw July in one hard flash: the kitchen, the bed, Dominic's name in her mouth.

"I'm sorry," he said.

She let out a breath that wasn't quite a laugh. "That isn't enough."

No. It wasn't.

He went back into the bedroom. He was not going to tell her tonight. He got under the covers and stayed on his own half of the mattress.

Out in the living room the couch springs gave once as she shifted. He pictured the seam pressing into her hip, then pulled the blanket up and did not get up again.
state_deltas_required: The double bind of being both the architect of Mort's destruction and the stranger who promised to help — visiting his father's store in the body of the man whose signature is on the demolition order

Nate enters the hardware store and makes a promise to his father that may be impossible to keep. The deception is no longer just personal survival — it's now an active relationship built on a lie that Nate cannot sustain or abandon.
